items.title: Choose join type deliberately
items.avoid: Defaulting to inner merge because it 'cleans' NaN without counting dropped rows.
items.prefer: Left-merge from the modeling grain (events, users) and report how many rows lost matches; use inner only when unmatched rows are invalid by definition.
items.why: Row drops from inner joins skew training distribution toward entities present in every table.
items.example: `pd.merge(events, dim, on='id', how='left'); assert len(out) == len(events)` when events must be preserved.

items.title: Validate join cardinality
items.avoid: Assuming one row per key on both sides without checks.
items.prefer: Run `df[key].duplicated().any()`, use `validate='m:1'` or `'1:1'`, and compare `len(merged)` to `len(left)`.
items.why: Many-to-many explosions duplicate samples and break independence assumptions in splits.
items.example: `pd.merge(users, tags, on='user_id', validate='m:1')` raises if tags has duplicate user_id rows.

items.title: Split before entity-level aggregations
items.avoid: Global groupby → merge → split workflows for ML features.
items.prefer: Split (or CV fold) → aggregate on train → map to train and test; use out-of-fold schemes for target-dependent stats.
items.why: Test partitions must not influence group-level statistics joined back as features.
items.example: Train-only `train.groupby('store_id')['sales'].mean()` then `.map` onto test rows—never `transform` on concatenated train+test.

items.title: Reset index and name join keys explicitly
items.avoid: Chaining groupby results with ambiguous MultiIndex columns into merge.
items.prefer: Use `as_index=False` or `reset_index()` so join keys are plain columns with stable names before merge.
items.why: Index misalignment causes silent wrong joins or duplicate column suffixes (_x, _y) that break pipelines.
